title: Power Monitoring and Electrical Load Analysis (Meraki MT)
dataSources: `index=meraki sourcetype=meraki:sensorreadingshistory metric="realPower"` from the `cisco_meraki_sensor_readings_history` input in `Splunk_TA_cisco_meraki` (Splunkbase 5580). Key fields: `serial`, `network.name`, `metric`, `realPower.draw` (watts), `voltage.level`, `current.draw`.
spl:
index=meraki sourcetype="meraki:sensorreadingshistory" metric="realPower"
| rename "realPower.draw" as power_w
| stats avg(power_w) as avg_power_w, max(power_w) as peak_power_w by serial, "network.name"
| eval power_capacity_pct=round(peak_power_w*100/15000, 2)
| sort - peak_power_w
description: Tracks electrical power consumption and load to identify anomalies and plan upgrades.
value: Tracks electrical power consumption and load to identify anomalies and plan upgrades.
implementation: Configure `cisco_meraki_sensor_readings_history` for MT40 power monitor sensors. `realPower.draw` is in watts; the 15000 W denominator above is a standard 15A/120V circuit headroom ‚Äî adjust to match the rated capacity of the monitored circuit.
visualization: Power consumption gauge; peak load timeline; capacity planning chart.
dataModelAcceleration: Not applicable (no CIM or Operational_Telemetry data model in this SPL).
knownFalsePositives: Benign events during building tests, after-hours schedules, and vendor work‚Äîconfirm against the BMS console before urgent response.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
‚Ä¢ Install and configure the required add-on or app: `Cisco Meraki Add-on for Splunk` (Splunkbase 5580).
‚Ä¢ Ensure the following data sources are available: `index=meraki sourcetype=meraki:sensorreadingshistory metric="realPower"` from the `cisco_meraki_sensor_readings_history` input in `Splunk_TA_cisco_meraki` (Splunkbase 5580). Key fields: `serial`, `network.name`, `metric`, `realPower.draw` (watts), `voltage.level`, `current.draw`..
‚Ä¢ For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 ‚Äî Configure data collection
Configure `cisco_meraki_sensor_readings_history` for MT40 power monitor sensors. `realPower.draw` is in watts; the 15000 W denominator above is a standard 15A/120V circuit headroom ‚Äî adjust to match the rated capacity of the monitored circuit.

Step 2 ‚Äî Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=meraki sourcetype="meraki:sensorreadingshistory" metric="realPower"
| rename "realPower.draw" as power_w
| stats avg(power_w) as avg_power_w, max(power_w) as peak_power_w by serial, "network.name"
| eval power_capacity_pct=round(peak_power_w*100/15000, 2)
| sort - peak_power_w
```

Understanding this SPL

**Power Monitoring and Electrical Load Analysis (Meraki MT)** ‚Äî Tracks electrical power consumption and load to identify anomalies and plan upgrades.

Documented **Data sources**: `index=meraki sourcetype=meraki:sensorreadingshistory metric="realPower"` from the `cisco_meraki_sensor_readings_history` input in `Splunk_TA_cisco_meraki` (Splunkbase 5580). Key fields: `serial`, `network.name`, `metric`, `realPower.draw` (watts), `voltage.level`, `current.draw`. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): `Cisco Meraki Add-on for Splunk` (Splunkbase 5580). The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed‚Äîrename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: meraki; **sourcetype**: meraki:sensorreadingshistory. That sourcetype matches what this use case lists under Data sources.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

‚Ä¢ Scopes the data: index=meraki, sourcetype="meraki:sensorreadingshistory". Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
‚Ä¢ Renames fields with `rename` for clarity or joins.
‚Ä¢ `stats` rolls up events into metrics; results are split **by serial, "network.name"** so each row reflects one combination of those dimensions (useful for per-host, per-user, or per-entity comparisons for this use case).
‚Ä¢ `eval` defines or adjusts **power_capacity_pct** ‚Äî often to normalize units, derive a ratio, or prepare for thresholds.
‚Ä¢ Orders rows with `sort` ‚Äî combine with `head`/`tail` for top-N patterns.


Step 3 ‚Äî Validate
Confirm that events are present in the index and that the search returns expected results. Compare with known good/bad scenarios if applicable. Verify field extractions and index permissions.

Step 4 ‚Äî Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Power consumption gauge; peak load timeline; capacity planning chart.
grandmaExplanation: We watch how your building systems behave. We help you know if something is off with this area: power monitoring and electrical load analysis before harm, damage, or wasted effort piles up.
